refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total operational time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement for remote equipment control. Refinement clarifies the measurement basis (bidirectional link), acceptance threshold (≥0.999), and evaluation scope (agreed period). High safety criticality demands explicit availability targets to prevent loss-of-control scenarios.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) with timestamped logs; calculation of availability as (total_uptime / total_evaluation_period); comparison against 0.999 threshold; root-cause analysis of downtime events.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall implement redundant communication paths to maintain availability.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall log all link state changes and availability metrics for audit.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or failover upon link loss to maintain safety.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dual-path network architecture (primary + backup) with automatic failover and continuous availability monitoring.
DP-B: Deploy redundant communication modules with load balancing and health-check protocols.
DP-C: Establish network link quality assurance via SLA-backed carrier service with contractual availability guarantees.
DP-D: Combine multi-path routing, local link state detection, and automated failover with real-time telemetry feedback.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Combine multi-path routing, local link state detection, and automated failover with real-time telemetry feedback to achieve ≥0.999 availability through redundancy, rapid detection, and graceful degradation.

potential_sources: Link detection logic must couple with Safety Services to trigger safe-state commands on loss.
Failover mechanism must couple with Vehicle control to ensure smooth transition without command loss.
Telemetry feedback loop couples with Teleoperation module to validate link health.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is acceptable and necessary; dependencies are well-defined and manageable at the IM level. No circular or hidden dependencies detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network infrastructure supports dual-path routing with <100ms failover latency.
Assumption: Vehicle and Teleoperation modules can tolerate brief command buffering during failover.
Risk: Failover latency may exceed safety-critical response windows if not carefully tuned.
Risk: Multi-path routing adds complexity; misconfiguration could degrade availability rather than improve it.
Risk: Real-time telemetry feedback introduces additional network load, potentially reducing effective bandwidth.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify failover latency budget (e.g., <50ms) and telemetry sampling rate to balance detection speed with network load. Add requirement for failover testing and validation protocol.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the teleoperation link availability logic, including multi-path routing, link state detection, failover orchestration, and telemetry feedback. Interfaces with Vehicle for command delivery, Safety Services for safe-state triggers, and external Network Link for physical connectivity.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and mission tasking from tele-operator; Teleoperation module buffers and prioritizes commands during failover.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, link health metrics, and availability logs to Site Manager and monitoring systems; includes link state transitions and failover events.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints from Safety Services; sends link-loss alerts and triggers safe-state commands via Safety Services on link failure.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Manages dual-path network connectivity; monitors link quality, initiates failover, and reports link state to internal detection logic.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic, failover orchestration, and telemetry feedback. High containment; minimal diffusion to Vehicle (command delivery) and Safety Services (safe-state triggers).
Safety Services should not own link availability; it should only react to link-loss alerts. High diffusion risk if availability logic is scattered across Safety Services.
Vehicle module should not own teleoperation link availability; it should only execute commands. Ownership here would create tight coupling and diffusion of network logic into vehicle control.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but too coarse-grained for detailed link availability logic. Ownership here would diffuse responsibility and obscure accountability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because it directly manages the command-telemetry link, has visibility into both paths, and can orchestrate failover without requiring changes to Vehicle or Safety Services logic. Interfaces are clean and unidirectional (except SafetyAlert, which is a necessary bidirectional safety coupling).
containment_risks: Risk: If Teleoperation module becomes too large, link availability logic may diffuse into sub-components; recommend clear internal separation of concerns (detection, failover, telemetry).
Risk: Tight coupling with Network Link interface; if network abstraction changes, Teleoperation must adapt. Mitigate via stable interface contract.

Risk: SafetyAlert bidirectional coupling introduces potential for circular dependencies if Safety Services also monitors link state; recommend clear ownership: Teleoperation detects, Safety Services reacts.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the internal architecture of Teleoperation module to separate link detection, failover logic, and telemetry reporting into distinct sub-components. Define explicit interface contracts for Network Link and SafetyAlert to prevent diffusion and circular dependencies.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (multi-path routing + link detection + failover + telemetry feedback) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 availability). The DP is specific, measurable, and technically feasible. No independence violations detected; the DP is a coherent solution to the FR.

diffusion_risks: Failover orchestration logic must remain in Teleoperation; if scattered across Vehicle or Safety Services, availability guarantees become unverifiable.
Telemetry feedback loop must be owned by Teleoperation to ensure consistent availability metrics; diffusion to multiple modules would create conflicting measurements.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module provides strong containment (0.95 score) with minimal diffusion. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and necessary. No hidden or implicit dependencies detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability can be verified via continuous monitoring of link state transitions and timestamped logs. Teleoperation module can expose availability metrics via Telemetry interface. Acceptance criteria (≥0.999) is measurable and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Teleoperation module is clearly responsible for achieving the availability target. Vehicle and Safety Services have well-defined reactive roles (execute commands, enforce safe states). Ownership is unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify failover latency budget and telemetry sampling rate.
Define explicit interface contracts for Network Link and SafetyAlert to prevent diffusion.
Add sub-FR for link detection latency (FR-NGHE-010.1) with acceptance criteria.
Document assumptions about network infrastructure and failover testing protocol.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (link detection, redundancy, logging, graceful degradation). Each sub-FR requires further refinement and DP selection. The selected DP-D is sound and well-contained in Teleoperation, but the sub-FRs must be decomposed to the next level to ensure completeness and traceability. High safety criticality demands rigorous decomposition of all availability mechanisms.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds and trigger failover.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall maintain dual communication paths with automatic switchover.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall log all link state changes, failover events, and availability metrics for audit and root-cause analysis.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or failover upon link loss while maintaining safety invariants (e.g., vehicle stops, safe state enforced).

revise.why: DP-D should be refined to specify failover latency budget, telemetry sampling rate, and link detection algorithm. Current DP is directionally correct but lacks implementation detail needed for sub-level decomposition.

assumptions: Network infrastructure supports dual-path routing with <100ms failover latency.
Vehicle and Teleoperation modules can tolerate brief command buffering during failover.
Safety Services can enforce safe-state commands within [Y] milliseconds of link-loss alert.
Telemetry feedback loop does not introduce unacceptable latency or bandwidth overhead.
Link state detection is based on heartbeat/keepalive protocol with configurable timeout.
open_risks: Failover latency may exceed safety-critical response windows if not carefully tuned.
Multi-path routing adds complexity; misconfiguration could degrade availability.
Real-time telemetry feedback introduces additional network load.
Circular dependency risk between Teleoperation and Safety Services if both monitor link state.
Network Link interface contract must be stable; changes could require Teleoperation refactoring.
evidence_needed: Network infrastructure assessment: confirm dual-path capability and failover latency.
Failover testing protocol: define test scenarios, acceptance criteria, and validation procedures.
Link detection algorithm specification: heartbeat interval, timeout threshold, detection latency.
Safety Services interface contract: define safe-state command format and response time.
Telemetry sampling rate analysis: balance detection speed with network load.
Availability measurement methodology: define evaluation period, uptime calculation, and audit trail.
